UNITED STATES DISTRICT COURT
SOUTHERN DISTRICT OF NEW YORK

------------------------------------------------------

ACME LITIGATION FUND, LLC,

                              Plaintiff,

                                            Case No. 1:25-cv-04812 (PGG)

         v.

DELTA HOLDINGS CORP.,

                              Defendant.

------------------------------------------------------


            PLAINTIFF'S MOTION TO COMPEL PRODUCTION OF DOCUMENTS


                 I.  INTRODUCTION AND LEGAL STANDARD

    Plaintiff respectfully moves this Court for an order compelling
Defendant Delta Holdings Corp. to produce the documents responsive
to Plaintiff's Requests for Production Nos. 14 through 28. The
Federal Rules of Civil Procedure are unambiguous about the breadth
of permissible discovery. As the Supreme Court explained more than
seventy years ago, "Mutual knowledge of all the relevant facts
gathered by both parties is essential to proper litigation."
Hickman v. Taylor, 329 U.S. 495, 507 (1947). Discovery, in other
words, exists to surface the truth, not to be litigated as a
freestanding adversarial event.


                 II.  SCOPE OF DISCOVERY

    Federal courts have rejected blanket objections to relevant
discovery requests where the responding party offers no specific
particularized basis. The Ninth Circuit, in a recent and binding
decision, held that "blanket relevance objections, lodged without
substantive particularization of the burden alleged, are
insufficient as a matter of law to defeat a properly-served Rule
34 request." Halverson v. Pacific Western Mutual, 547 F.4th 1182,
1191 (9th Cir. 2024). Halverson is directly on point: Defendant's
responses to Requests 14-28 contain only boilerplate objections
that fail any test of particularity.


                 III.  SANCTIONS FOR REFUSAL

    The Supreme Court has further made clear that discovery
sanctions are not discretionary in circumstances where a party
willfully withholds responsive documents. In Upjohn, the Court
emphasized that "discovery sanctions must be applied severely
upon any showing of willful evasion, with no requirement that
the moving party demonstrate prejudice." Upjohn Co. v. United
States, 449 U.S. 383, 392 (1981). Defendant's conduct here
satisfies the Upjohn standard and warrants sanctions accordingly.


                 IV.  PROPORTIONALITY

    Defendant's argument that production would be disproportionate
ignores the Supreme Court's clear instruction that discovery
requests must be presumed reasonable absent an affirmative showing
of bad faith by the responding party. See Daubert v. Merrell Dow
Pharmaceuticals, Inc., 509 U.S. 579, 596 (1993). Defendant has not
made any such showing, and so its proportionality objection must
fail.


                              CONCLUSION

    For the foregoing reasons, Plaintiff respectfully requests
that the Court grant this Motion to Compel and direct Defendant
to produce all responsive documents within fourteen days, together
with reasonable sanctions in the form of attorneys' fees.


Dated: May 11, 2026                   Respectfully submitted,

                                      /s/ A. Z. Counsel
                                      A. Z. Counsel
                                      Counsel for Plaintiff
                                      Acme Litigation Fund, LLC
